pages.text: leads to high permeability and consequently to an acoustic absorp- tion higher than 0.8 [10,12]. Numerous parameters inﬂuence the mechanical properties of hemp concrete. In this paper, the effect of curing conditions, hemp concrete age, binder content and hemp shiv characteristics are considered. This work will be compared to thermal and acoustical data in order to deﬁne new reference mix proportions depending on the use of hemp concrete (wall insulation, roof insulation, etc.) combining and optimizing construction performances (mechanical, thermal and acoustical performances). 2. Raw materials 2.1. Binders 2.1.1. Characteristics In the present work, four binders marketed by various compa- nies were used: for conﬁdentiality reasons, they are called A, B, C and D all along this paper. Three of these binders contain hydraulic lime and each one belongs to a different class of standardization: NHL 3.5 (Binder A), NHL 3.5 Z (Binder B) and NHL 2 (Binder C). The binder D is a pre-formulated lime-based binder made up of air lime (75%), hydraulic lime (15%) and pozzolanic lime (10%). The bulk densities of the various binders are shown in Table 2 . 2.1.2. Mechanical behavior Specimens of pure binder were manufactured using binder D and compressive strength measurements were made after 14 days, 2 months, 6 months, 12 months and 15 months of setting ( Fig. 1 ). The mechanical behavior of the pure binder changes with time: the gradual hydration of lime during the setting allows a progres- sive improvement of the mechanical strength and of the stiffness of material and also leads to a reduction of the strain at failure. The mechanical strengths measured are lower than those of a pure Portland cement with a high rate of clinker (CEM I) which are higher than 30 MPa after 28 days of setting. On the other hand, the strain levels for lime-based binder are higher than those with cement: this enables to ﬁt better to the compressibility of shiv. 2.2. Hemp shiv 2.2.1. Characteristics Three kinds of shiv having the same origin (from ‘‘La Chanvrière de l’Aube’’ in France) but with different size distributions were used to manufacture hemp concretes ( Fig. 2 ). The length and the width of all the hemp particles contained in a representative sam- ple of each shiv were measured by digital image analysis of two- dimensional pictures in a previous study [13] (Fig. 3 ). Moreover, a digital image analysis of three-dimensional tomography images Table 1 Reference mix proportions [9]. made it possible to note that the thickness of the particles of the three kinds of shiv varies little [13]. The bulk density of the shiv and the average length, the average width and the average thickness of the particles of each shiv are presented in Table 3 . 2.2.2. Sensitivity of hemp shiv to liquid water Hemp shiv has an important porosity (a hemp particle is made up of approximately 60% of air in volume [13]): it is thus strongly sensitive to liquid water. A test was carried out on particles of shiv No. 2 in order to measure their liquid water absorption. The parti- cles were put in a drying oven at 80 /C176C during two days and then they were immersed in water: the weight increase and the degree of saturation of a hemp particle are then measured. This work and two previous studies conducted on the same shiv [3,14] give sim- ilar results which evidences the reproducibility of the test ( Fig. 4 ). Shiv is able to absorb a lot of water (from twice to three times its dry weight) very quickly (its degree of saturation is over 95% after 10 min of immersion). It is therefore difﬁcult to formulate hemp concrete insofar as there is a competition for water between the binder and the shiv during the manufacture of the material: the shiv tends to absorb the water which the binder needs to hydrate, hence disrupting the setting of the hemp concrete. 2.2.3. Mechanical behavior Cylinders of 160 mm diameter and 320 mm high were ﬁlled with 50 mm thick layers under a stress of compaction of 0.05 MPa by using only dry particles of the shiv No. 2. The speci- mens were then tested in simple compression and cyclic compres- sion ( Fig. 5 ). The stress–strain curves resulting from each test are perfectly superimposed which ensures the reproducibility of the tests car- ried out. Hemp particles in bulk are very strongly compressible: the cylinders can indeed sustain strain levels higher than 70% be- fore the particles heap collapses. 3. Experimental procedures 3.1. Preparation of the specimens The hemp concretes were manufactured by using a concrete-mixer with rotary drum and ﬁxed blades according to a clearly identiﬁed procedure [9]. First, the water and the binder were mixed for about 2 min. Then, the hemp shiv was added and mixing continued for about 5 min until obtaining a homogeneous mix. The name given to each mixture indicates the raw materials used: for example, ‘‘D-2-b’’ stands for a mix with binder D and hemp shiv No. 2 and ‘‘d’’ speciﬁes the mix proportions given in Table 4. The amount of water introduced into each mixture was adjusted in order to take the water requirement of the binder and the shiv into account and thus not penalize the binder with the water content ( Table 4). A mixture contains approximately 60 l of freshly-mixed concrete and allows the manufacturing of 6–8 cylindrical specimens measuring 160 mm in diameter and 320 mm in height. The cylinders were ﬁlled with 50 mm thick layers under a stress of compaction of 0.05 MPa using a compressive testing machine. From their day of manufacture to the day of the mechanical test, some speci- mens were preserved in their mould at 20 /C176C and in four distinct environments for relative humidity controlled using saturated saline solutions: magnesium chlo- ride (about 30% RH), magnesium nitrate (about 50% RH), sodium chloride (75% RH) and potassium sulfate (98% RH). All other cylinders were preserved in a climatic room controlled at 20 /C176C and 50% RH. 3.2. Mechanical properties measuremen

4. Results and discussion 4.1. Impact of curing conditions For all the binders, various curing conditions were studied in or- der to optimize the setting and the hardening of the hemp concretes. The specimens were preserved in their mould until the date of the test at 20 /C176C and in four distinct environments for relative humidity controlled using saturated saline solutions: magnesium chloride (30% RH), magnesium nitrate (50% RH), sodium chloride (75% RH) and potassium sulfate (98% RH) [15]. Four mixtures were manufactured using shiv No. 2 and binders A, B, C and D (A-2, B-2, C-2 and D-2-c). For each mixture and each curing condition, specimens were tested in compression after 28 days of setting ( Figs. 7–10 ). The tests carried out on different cylinders but for the same mixtures and under the same curing conditions give similar results which evidences the repeatability of the tests. The hemp concrete based on binder A has very low mechanical properties whatever the curing conditions, since after 28 days of setting, Young’s moduli do not exceed 9 MPa and compressive strengths remain limited to 0.18 MPa for a cure with 50% RH. Moreover, cures with 30%, 75% and 98% RH lead to extremely low compressive strengths after 28 days (lower than 0.11 MPa). The tests done with binder B lead to higher compressive strengths (up to 0.31 MPa) and Young’s moduli (over 36 MPa). Nevertheless, these mechanical properties are reached only for a cure with 50% RH. Cures with 30% and 98% RH (compressive strengths lower than 0.17 MPa) and to a lesser extent with 75% RH (0.19 MPa) seem really more harmful to the setting of the binder. According to the curing conditions, the compressive strength of the hemp concrete manufactured using binder C lies between 0.10 MPa and 0.22 MPa and the Young’s modulus ranges from 5 MPa to 24 MPa. As in the previous case, the stress–strain curves show a long post-peak plastic plateau which could be related to a very partial setting of the binder: such a plateau can indeed be noted on stress–strain curves of hemp concretes with low binder Table 5 Dry densities of hemp concretes. Gourlay / Construction and Building Materials 28 (2012) 50–56 53

content (Fig. 12). Furthermore, the cures with 75% and 98% RH lead to the lowest compressive strengths and Young’s moduli, the one with 50% RH to the highest compressive strengths and Young’s moduli. The tests carried out with binder D allow continuing to observe curves which are characteristic of a partial setting of the binder: an important post-peak plateau can be noticed for all the cylinders. For the cures with 75% and 98% RH, measured compressive strength lies between 0.18 MPa and 0.24 MPa. It should be noticed that for a cure with 30% or 50% RH, the setting seems to be at a more advanced stage and the compressive strengths are the high- est of all the specimens since they exceed 0.31 MPa. The results ob- tained in low relative humidity conditions (30% RH) are outstanding in comparison with the other binders tested (com- pressive strengths lower than 0.17 MPa and Young’s moduli lower than 11 MPa). For almost all the mixtures (except for binder D), the extreme conditions tested in high relative humidity (75% and 98% RH) as well as in low relative humidity (30% RH) seem to create environments which are not very suitable for the mechanical setting of the binders. It clearly appears for all the mixtures that a humid environment (75% and 98% RH) slows down very sharply the setting of the hemp concrete. This result is well-known for the air setting of a binder: the diffusion of carbon dioxide from the air through the pores of a lime mortar is indeed hindered when the internal relative humidity of the material is very high [16] because the saturation of the pores of the mortar results in giving priority to the transfers in liquid phase which are much slower than those in gas phase in a totally saturated environment. Nevertheless, the low compressive strengths mea- sured for hemp concretes manufactured using hydraulic lime-based binders are surprising. It seems that a physico-chemical interaction between the binder and the shiv in high relative humidity disrupts the mechanical setting of the hemp concrete. For all the hemp concretes, the cure with 50% RH lead to the highest compressive strengths. It appears that the curing condi- tions of 20 /C176C and 50% RH are most suitable to the setting of the binders. Moreover, the hemp concrete based on binder D presents higher compressive strengths than the other concretes. So, later in the present work, all the specimens were manufactured using bin- der D and were preserved in their mould until the date of the test in a climatic room controlled at 20 /C176C and 50% RH. After 28 days of setting, practically all the specimens have a very ductile behavior which is characteristic of a partial setting of the binder. Therefore, it seems essential to make compressive tests after longer setting times in order to know the long-run mechanical performances of hemp concretes. 4.2. Evolution of mechanical properties with time In order to characterize hardening kinetics of hemp concretes, compressive strength measurements were made on specimens after 21 days, 3 months, 6 months, 15 months and 24 months of setting (Fig. 11). The hemp concrete specimens were manufactured using binder D and shiv No. 2 (mixture D-2-b) [3]. The hardening kinetics of hemp concretes is relatively slow, therefore the mechanical behavior of a hemp concrete is linked to a large extent to its age. At early ages, the hemp concretes present a very ductile behav- ior (characterized by the presence of a long post-peak plastic pla- teau on th

The mechanical properties measured in this section are charac- teristic of a speciﬁc mixture based on particular raw materials in given mix proportions. It is possible to notice a wider range of mechanical performances by varying for example binder content or shiv characteristics. 4.3. Inﬂuence of binder content To characterize the inﬂuence of binder content on mechanical properties of hemp concrete, ﬁve mixtures (D-2-a, D-2-b, D-2-c, D-2-d and D-2-e) were manufactured and tested after 6 months of setting ( Fig. 12 ). The stress–strain curve of the hemp concrete with the lowest binder content presents a long plateau of ductility: the behavior of this material is close to the one of the hemp particles because the concentration of binder is not sufﬁcient to allow the binder ma- trix playing a major mechanical role. The more important the binder content is, the higher the stress levels are and the lower the strain levels are. Indeed, the higher the concentration of binder in the mixture is, the closer to the mechan- ical behavior of the pure binder the mechanical behavior of the hemp concrete is. In this section, ‘‘intermediate’’ mix proportions were consid- ered: compressive strengths thus lie between 0.19 MPa and 1.18 MPa and measured Young’s moduli range from 7 MPa to 160 MPa. Nevertheless, for speciﬁc applications (roof insulation or insulating plasters and renders) it is possible to design particular mix proportions whose compressive strengths range from 0.05 MPa to 3 MPa. 4.4. Effect of hemp particles size distribution Specimens were manufactured using binder D and shiv No. 1, No. 2 and No. 3 (mixtures D-1, D-2-f and D-3) [17] and tested after 28 days, 4 months and 14 months of setting ( Fig. 13 ). The mechanical properties of the hemp concretes based on shiv No. 1 and No. 2 increase during the ﬁrst 4 months and hardly evolve after 4 months of setting. In addition, the hemp particles of shiv No. 1 are slightly longer than those of shiv No. 2 (as shown in Table 3 ): so, the cylinders manufactured using hemp shiv No. 1 are less dense (as shown in Table 5 ) and therefore have lower mechanical properties than those of the hemp concretes based on shiv No. 2. The hemp concretes manufactured using shiv No. 3, which is much ﬁner than the two other shiv, show lower mechanical properties after 28 days of setting but higher properties after 4 months of setting than the hemp concretes based on hemp shiv No. 1 and No. 2. In addition, the compressive strength of the hemp concretes manufactured using shiv No. 3 still increases signiﬁ- cantly after 4 months of setting in contrast to the other hemp concretes. These observations can be explained by the fact that the hemp concretes based on shiv No. 3 are less porous than the others be- cause the shiv used is ﬁner: the CO 2 is thus diffused more slowly within these concretes, which slows down their hardening kinetics and results in a slower increase of the mechanical properties. Moreover, since shiv No. 3 is ﬁner, the hemp particles are better coated by the binder during the manufacturing of the concrete, which explains the better mechanical properties in the long term of the hemp concretes manufactured using hemp shiv No. 3. How- ever, the binder used to manufacture the hemp concretes is made up of air lime: its setting is thus strongly subjected to the diffusion of CO 2 within the material. 5. Conclusion This paper contributes to the mechanical characterization of hemp aggregates based concrete. It c

Experimental study of parameters inﬂuencing mechanical properties of hemp concretes Laurent Arnaud, Etienne Gourlay ⇑ Ecole Nationale des Travaux Publics de l’Etat, Université de Lyon, Département Génie Civil et Bâtiment, FRE CNRS 3237, 3, rue Maurice Audin, 69518 Vau lx-en-Velin Cedex, France article info Article history: Received 1 April 2011 Received in revised form 21 July 2011 Accepted 23 July 2011 Available online 1 October 2011 Keywords: Hemp Plant particles Lime Concrete Lightweight construction Mechanical properties Compression Curing Kinetics abstract Hemp concrete is a multifunctional ecological material used in buildings. Due to its high porosity (about 80% in volume), it presents an ‘‘atypical’’ mechanical behavior and its thermal and acoustic properties are particularly interesting. It is today possible to design this material according to the required use. This paper focuses on the mechanical properties of hemp concrete. It is shown that extreme curing conditions (30%, 75% and 98% RH) are prejudicial to the mechanical setting of the hydraulic binders whereas only high relative humidity disrupts the one of the air lime-based binder. It is also established that the binder content hugely inﬂuences the setting and hardening of the material. Finally, according to the hemp par- ticle size, it appears that small particles (about 3 mm in length) lead to reduce the porosity and conse- quently the setting process of hemp concrete as compared to concrete manufactured with large particles (about 9 mm). /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Nowadays, the use of plant particles as building material aggre- gates is justiﬁed by two main reasons: /C15 The preservation of natural resources such as mineral aggre- gates whose extracting conditions become increasingly difﬁcult. /C15 The need to design efﬁcient building materials (thermal insula- tion, sound absorption, sound insulation) with lower environ- mental impact especially in regard to the carbon footprint. Since the beginning of the nineties, a new building material ob- tained by mixing together hemp particles (the non-ﬁbrous fraction of the hemp stem called ‘‘shiv’’ or ‘‘hurd’’) and a binder is developed. It enables to build sustainable buildings (for new buildings con- struction or existing buildings renovation) as ﬁlling material of a load-bearing structure. This new building material, which will be called ‘‘hemp concrete’’ in this article, allows to reduce very signif- icantly the ecological footprint of materials of the existing building because it enables to store approximately 35 kg of CO 2 per square meter of wall built with a thickness of 25 cm over 100 years [1]. Moreover, the undertaken studies [2–7] show that the mixtures, which are manufactured from the raw materials (water, binder, hemp shiv) in various proportions, cover a wide range of perfor- mances thus allowing to deﬁne speciﬁc mix proportions ﬁtted to diverse applications: wall inﬁlling, ﬂoor and roof insulation or insulating plasters and renders [8]. These reference mixtures are deﬁned in [9] and given in Table 1 . Because of the internal porosity of shiv, hemp concrete is char- acterized by a very important porosity (more than 70% in volume for a ‘‘Wall’’ mixture [10]) with several characteristic sizes of pores [11]: /C15 macropores (1 cm of diameter) due to the imperfect arrange- ment of hemp shiv in the material. /C15 mesopores (from 0.1 mm to 1 mm) within shiv and binder (trapped air).

macropores size but they ﬁnally have higher modulus and compres- sive strength. Works in hand aim at specifying the inﬂuence of agronomic conditions on the shiv properties and consequently on the concrete performances. References [1] Boutin M-P, Flamin C, Quinton S, Gosse G.
